feed.title: blank
feed.subtitle: A simple, whitespace theme for academics. Based on [*folio](https://github.com/bogoli/-folio) design.

entry.title: Does Physics AI halluciate?: Two necessary safeguards for engineering decision making with Physics AI.

link.title: Does Physics AI halluciate?: Two necessary safeguards for engineering decision making with Physics AI.

entry.content:
Does Physics AI Hallucinate?

Yes, it does.

At the end of the day, a trained neural network is a series of linear calculations and activation functions. There is no mechanism that will make it fail—unless the input is out of distribution or out of floating-point range. It will always give you something. And most of the time, that something will look plausible.

The Quiet Danger of Plausible Nonsense

Say you have a model trained on a midsize SUV for external aerodynamics. What happens if you run a full-size pickup truck through the model? You’ll get a pressure field. You’ll get a drag coefficient. The output will look like a perfectly reasonable CFD result.

But would you use that inference for an engineering decision?

In conventional CFD, unreliable results announce themselves—divergence, poor residuals, unphysical flow features that experienced engineers can spot. Machine learning surrogates offer no such courtesy. A prediction on an out-of-distribution geometry is visually indistinguishable from one on in-distribution data. The model is confidently wrong, and it has no idea.

Physics AI Needs Guardrails

Without proper guardrails, any pipeline built on Physics AI carries a high risk of failure at validation—the point where you test the ML-informed engineering design against real CAE simulation. That’s wasted time, wasted compute, and wasted engineering effort.

Proper guardrails can save valuable resources by:

  1. Filtering out meaningless inferences before you commit to acting on them, and
  2. Adding confidence intervals to the inference results so engineers know where to trust—and where not to.

Bracketing Inference with Uncertainty

At General Motors Research and Development, we have a (in terms of SciML timeline) long history with Scientific Machine Learning and Physics AI. Based on our experience deploying neural surrogates for production vehicle aerodynamics, we came to a clear conclusion: it is time to look at the uncertainties.

We examined two UQ methods and propose sandwiching any inference between them:

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  1. PRE-INFERENCE: Is this geometry in-domain?  │
│     → Mahalanobis Distance (OOD Detection)      │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  2. INFERENCE                                   │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  3. POST-INFERENCE: How confident is this?      │
│     → MC Dropout (Confidence Intervals)         │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Stage 1: Before You Predict—Is This Geometry Even Appropriate?

Before committing GPU-hours to inference, a single forward pass through the model’s geometry encoder extracts a learned feature vector. We then compute the Mahalanobis distance between that vector and the training distribution’s statistics. If the distance exceeds the training distribution’s threshold, the geometry is flagged as out-of-distribution. Full stop. Run a CFD simulation instead.

In our experiments across 511 vehicle simulations spanning five vehicle types, this approach provided zero-overlap separation between seen and unseen vehicle types. The unseen vehicle scored 3× higher than any training sample—despite its drag coefficient and frontal area overlapping with the training vehicles in conventional scalar metrics. The feature space sees what Cd × Area plots cannot.

Even within the in-distribution regime, higher Mahalanobis distances consistently correlated with larger prediction errors—making it not just a binary gate, but a continuous reliability indicator.

Cost: One forward pass + a matrix–vector product. Negligible.

Stage 2: After You Predict—Where Should You Trust It?

For geometries that pass the OOD screen, MC Dropout provides spatially resolved uncertainty maps. By running 30 stochastic forward passes with dropout active at inference time, we obtain a distribution of predictions at every surface point. The variance tells you where on the vehicle the model is least confident.

The spatial signal is real: high-variance regions coincide with high-error regions (front fascia, wheel arches, underbody separation zones). This gives engineers actionable guidance—”look more carefully at this area”—rather than a single number for the whole vehicle.

An important caveat: The absolute confidence intervals from MC Dropout are severely miscalibrated. Our 95% CI achieved actual coverage of only 3.5–47.7% depending on the force component. The model is systematically overconfident. MC Dropout tells you where to worry, but not how much to worry. We report this honestly because practitioners need to know the limitations before deploying any UQ method.

What We Learned

  Mahalanobis Distance MC Dropout
Purpose Pre-inference OOD screening Post-inference confidence
Cost 1 forward pass 30× forward passes
Output Single score per geometry Spatially resolved field
OOD detection Direct, unambiguous Indirect (elevated σ)
Calibration N/A (it’s a score) Poor (overconfident)
Practical value High—deploy on every sample Moderate—reserve for critical decisions

The Takeaway

The contribution here is not algorithmic—neither Mahalanobis distance nor MC Dropout is new. The point is that SciML inference should be bracketed by uncertainty quantification, and that such a pipeline can be assembled from off-the-shelf components under realistic industrial constraints: limited simulation budgets, proprietary geometries, and models with high-dimensional feature spaces.

As neural surrogates move from benchmark papers toward production engineering tools, uncertainty quantification is not an optional add-on. It is the missing piece that turns an impressive demo into a deployable system.

The pipeline structure—pre-inference OOD screening followed by post-inference confidence estimation—is not specific to aerodynamics. Any SciML surrogate that maps geometry to physical fields faces the same risk of silent extrapolation. Crashworthiness, thermal management, structural analysis—wherever data-driven surrogates support engineering decisions, they need guardrails.

Preprint: Bracketing Inference with Uncertainty Quantification: A Reliability Pipeline for Neural Aerodynamic Surrogates

Authors: Seunghwan Keum, Alok Warey — General Motors Research and Development

entry.title: Transfer Learning in Scientific Machine Learning: A key to the data problem.

link.title: Transfer Learning in Scientific Machine Learning: A key to the data problem.

entry.content:
When training an AI/ML with CAE data, how can we minimize data generation and maximize return with expensive, scarce CAE data? Transfer Learning can be the breakthrough.

I’m attending the hashtag#NAFEMS Americas Conference for the first time. About a third of the talks, maybe more, are on AI/ML. Two clear trends stand out: hashtag#AgenticAI and hashtag#ScientificML (hashtag#PhysicsAI). I’ll save Agentic AI for another post. This one is about Scientific ML, where the progress has been remarkable. Architectures, both open-source and proprietary, have reached impressive levels of accuracy. UX and deployment have been refined to the point where end users no longer need to be AI/ML experts (a double-edged sword, but that’s a separate conversation). Yet one critical piece still demands careful consideration: training data generation.

Over the years, I keep getting the same questions: “How many CAE runs do we need to train a model?” “What if my development program can be done with fewer runs than what’s needed for training data?”

This is the ROI question that every engineering organization must answer before adopting SciML. In an industrial setting, we can’t run thousands of CAE simulations for the sake of model building for every single design. The cost parity doesn’t exist. On top of that, more data means more overhead for training (Data generation and training costs have been reviewed in an excellent article by Neil Ashton: https://lnkd.in/gQNVGiqF). So we need to be careful what we wish for: more data is a blessing and a curse. For SciML to scale in industry, we need a smarter way to maximize the return from expensive CAE simulations and maintain training cost under control.

Alok Warey and I tackled this head-on with Transfer Learning. The idea: take a model already trained on existing vehicle families and adapt it to a completely new, unseen vehicle with minimal additional data. We pretrained on four vehicle families, then fine-tuned for a fifth using just 30 CFD runs—compared to ~100 normally required.

We systematically compared three strategies: → Full Fine-Tuning → Lightweight (Partial) Fine-Tuning → Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA)

The differences were dramatic. Curious which one worked and why the others failed? Check out our preprint: https://lnkd.in/ggmSG9xq (Spoiler warning: see the figure.)

Transfer Learning offers a practical path to keeping training data generation to a minimum while maintaining prediction quality. One pretrained backbone, lightweight adapters per vehicle family, trained in 90% less time from 70% less data. And we just took the first step with geometry: think of all the other things we can fine-tune against.

On a related note: it is time to consider diversifying the open-source databases to cover various vehicle families. Any takers to work with us?
entry.summary: When training an AI/ML with CAE data, how can we minimize data generation and maximize return with expensive, scarce CAE data? Transfer Learning can be the breakthrough.

entry.title: Agentic SciML - No more Excel sheet to track my runs!

link.title: Agentic SciML - No more Excel sheet to track my runs!

entry.content:
The past few weeks have been crazy busy with an ongoing SciML project. I hope to share something interesting soon. In the meantime, I’ve been setting up “Vibe Physics” (https://lnkd.in/geQgPM7v) for my own use. I’ve been using VS Code + GitHub Copilot mostly for vibe coding, which has been working really well. Then, as I mentioned in a previous post, I came across Matthew Schwartz’s blog post about AI research assistants and decided to give it a try.

The idea is simple. My pipeline has four components: Python code, training config files in YAML format, a data folder containing various CFD simulation results, and a training/inference results folder. I prepared a copilot-instructions.md in each folder. In the code folder, I gave an overview of the codebase (along with a couple of papers in PDF format), described what each script does, and — since my training runs on HPC with LSF — explained my LSF script and its arguments. The config folder’s file includes a template YAML with the meaning of each key and a description of naming conventions. The data folder has a markdown describing which subfolder contains which dataset, since I have many different training sets.

Preparing these markdowns took considerable effort. For the code documentation, I asked Claude to analyze my codebase and write it up — that part was fairly straightforward. But I had to provide detailed context on the LSF queue setup and my submission script, which has grown over many years. I took time and care to document my training configurations and data collections as thoroughly as I could, and had Claude review everything afterward for consistency. The most time-consuming part was the inference results folder, where I described (again, with Claude’s help) all post-analysis files, mostly in CSV format.

I used to keep a couple of Excel files tracking my various runs — different datasets, hyperparameters, ablation studies, the whole nine yards. Even then, I frequently lost track of why a particular job was launched. Not anymore. Copilot now understands all the runs in my queue, monitors their progress, and generates a performance metric comparison when they complete. It makes recommendations based on its findings, and with a simple “Let’s do it,” it populates the config YAML files for the next iteration, submits jobs to the queue, and adds entries to the log markdown.

I have a very challenging project at hand, one that seemed impossible to meet on deadline just a few weeks ago. Matthew’s “AI grad student” has been a real force multiplier — boosting my productivity many times over. I can imagine the same principle applied to any engineering CAE workflow. The way we do CAE may look very different, soon. A quick snippet of post-analysis markdown is shown. I used to manually copy and paste all these numbers from many CSV files, and can’t imagine doing that again!
entry.summary: The past few weeks have been crazy busy with an ongoing SciML project. I hope to share something interesting soon. In the meantime, I’ve been setting up “Vibe Physics” (https://lnkd.in/geQgPM7v) for my own use. I’ve been using VS Code + GitHub Copilot mostly for vibe coding, which has been working really well. Then, as I mentioned in a previous post, I came across Matthew Schwartz’s blog post about AI research assistants and decided to give it a try.

entry.title: SAE WCX Recap: AI or Real?

link.title: SAE WCX Recap: AI or Real?

entry.content:
I had a great time presenting our latest work on the General Motors collaboration with NVIDIA PhysicsNeMO DoMINO evaluation at the SAE WCX external aerodynamics session today. A huge thank you to everyone who joined the discussion, and a special shout-out to Vishal Raul for pioneering the SciML vision at General Motors Research and Development, and the NVIDIA team for their incredible collaboration.

I closed my talk with the slide below. Yeah I know, it’s cheesy, but I thought this might be effective. Just a few years ago, we mocked AI generated images. Today, AI-generated images are nearly indistinguishable from reality.

I believe Scientific Machine Learning (SciML) is on a similar trajectory. The evolution from CNNs and GNNs to Transformer-based architectures like Neural Operators, and many other proprietary architectures actively developed by startups and ISVs are rapidly closing the gap between traditional CFD and AI-driven inference. SciML may not replace high-fidelity CFD, but the way we approach simulation and design optimization in a few years may look quite different from today, with SciML applications augmented by Agentic AI.

However, the elephant in the room remains: The training data. It is expensive, scarce, and siloed. We’ll need to come up with something to move forward with SciML.

I’d love to hear your thoughts on where SciML is headed. If you’re at hashtag#SAEWCX, let’s connect! I’ll be around tomorrow (4/16) as well. Feel free to DM me to catch up.
entry.summary: I had a great time presenting our latest work on the General Motors collaboration with NVIDIA PhysicsNeMO DoMINO evaluation at the SAE WCX external aerodynamics session today. A huge thank you to everyone who joined the discussion, and a special shout-out to Vishal Raul for pioneering the SciML vision at General Motors Research and Development, and the NVIDIA team for their incredible collaboration.

entry.title: The unique challenge in Scientific Machine Learning: Data

link.title: The unique challenge in Scientific Machine Learning: Data

entry.content:
Data is the king of any AI and Machine Learning task, but in the world of Scientific Machine Learning (SciML), it acts more like a tyrant. Honestly, I often feel the presence of Sauron in my data farm whenever I start a new project—that heavy, overbearing weight of massive datasets looming over everything.

What makes SciML data so unique, and frankly difficult, is its sheer individual size. While traditional ML might deal with millions of tiny files, a single data point in SciML can easily take up hundreds of megabytes to a couple of gigabytes. It’s always a bit funny when I tell people I’m training a model with “only” a hundred data points; they think it’s a toy problem until they realize those hundred points are hogging several terabytes of storage. When you scale that up to something like the massive external aerodynamics dataset recently showcased by PhysicsX with over 20,000 data points (https://lnkd.in/g7WBwjeu) , the orchestration challenge becomes mind-boggling.

Having this much data is a blessing for model accuracy but a curse for practical training. You simply can’t feed this beast with a single GPU, making HPC an absolute necessity. But then we face the ultimate bottleneck: how do we actually get that data to the GPUs? The old-school solution of using a single, massive data farm with an NFS mount is quickly becoming a relic of the past because the latency is just unbearable, especially in cloud-based systems.

The industry is pushing toward leveraging local scratch with high-speed NVMe tucked inside each compute node. And while modern NVMes offer many terabytes of capacity, they still can’t hold the entirety of a massive SciML training set. We need to actively “feed” the compute nodes—we need a sophisticated orchestration of the training data feed.

We can try Lazy Copy, prefetching at each epoch, or even look at solutions like Mosaic Streaming Dataset (MSD) that have revolutionized LLM training. However, SciML remains the outlier. Most streaming tools are optimized for billions of tiny samples, whereas in our world, a single data point might be larger than an entire shard in a typical LLM study.

So far, the research community in SciML has been rightfully obsessed with architecture—designing the next great PINN or Neural Operator. But as we move toward Foundation Models for physics, I believe it’s time to consider best practices for practical use. We need to start thinking deeply about how to orchestrate data management and training pipelines for these massive tensors, or we’ll find our fastest GPUs just sitting there, starving for data.

…and yes, as you might have guessed, I’ve been writing this post while waiting for my data to be copied to a local NVMe on my compute node from my data farm. I am the living proof of why data orchestration is so critical in SciML.

entry.title: Challenges in Applying SciML to Industrial CFD: The Data Bottleneck

link.title: Challenges in Applying SciML to Industrial CFD: The Data Bottleneck

entry.content:
While we have seen significant advancements in AI, particularly with LLMs, the adoption of Scientific Machine Learning (SciML) in fields like automotive aerodynamics faces unique challenges.

Reflecting on my recent work with SciML applications over the last year, I’ve found that the primary hurdle isn’t the algorithms—it remains the Data. While data quality is crucial for all AI models, the challenge in industrial CFD is far more nuanced than simple scarcity. There are excellent architectures available today (CNN, GNN, Neural Operators, Diffusion Models, Transformers, Mamba) that perform well on open-source benchmarks like DrivAerNet. However, bridging the gap between academic benchmarks and production reality is not straightforward.

  • The Gap Between Open-Source and Production Real-world production vehicles involve massive geometric variety and complexity—including underbody components, sensor brackets, and manufacturing constraints. Training a model that generalizes across this level of detail is difficult with clean, curated open-source datasets.

  • The ROI of Data Generation and Training Generating high-fidelity training data is costly. In a fast-paced production environment, running thousands of CFD simulations solely to train a model is hard to justify when standard project targets can be met with far fewer iterations.

  • The Complexity of Historical Data Leveraging historical CFD data is also challenging. Legacy data is often concentrated on specific design subspaces and can vary in turbulence models or mesh settings, creating inconsistencies that may confuse the model.

  • Moving Forward: Rethinking Data Efficiency We are seemingly stuck between the high cost of reliable data generation and the need for massive datasets to train robust models. Recently, an interesting idea of utilizing transient data was proposed in an insightful paper (arXiv:2511.20455). The authors suggest that instead of discarding intermediate steps, we can treat the physics during convergence as valuable training signals.

While this provides a promising direction, it raises further questions. Beyond utilizing transient flows, what are other effective ways to implement data augmentation in a physics-constrained environment? How do we maximize the value of the simulations we already run?

The future of industrial SciML may very well depend on it.
entry.summary: While we have seen significant advancements in AI, particularly with LLMs, the adoption of Scientific Machine Learning (SciML) in fields like automotive aerodynamics faces unique challenges.

entry.title: Scientific Machien Learning: Where are we going to ge all the data?

link.title: Scientific Machien Learning: Where are we going to ge all the data?

entry.content:
SciML (Scientific Machine Learning) comes with a unique set of “high-stakes” challenges. One is the sheer cost of data generation—requiring deep CAE expertise, massive compute, and time, as I mentioned in my previous post.

But there is another hurdle: Data Density. A single CFD or CAE data point can consist of millions of grid points. While these dense meshes are essential for solving the physics via traditional solvers, do we really need all of them for learning the physics?

I’m noticing a fascinating convergence in Transformer-based SciML architectures. The industry is moving away from “process everything at once” toward more strategic, sparse representations. NVIDIA DoMINO constructs computational stencils dynamically from randomly sampled points (https://lnkd.in/gynNsUSm). Emmi AI’s AB-UPT (Anchored-Branched Universal Physics Transformer, https://lnkd.in/gDzbWEpW ) was ahead of the curve in Transformer-based approaches, introducing supernodes and anchor points to handle massive datasets efficiently. We’ve seen similar logic in MSPT(https://lnkd.in/g2M53bkN) , ArGEnt (https://lnkd.in/gvX2BC7V), and SMART (https://lnkd.in/g8GEk3rZ). Now, Transolver has officially joined the ranks, adopting random sampling in its latest evolution Transolver-3 (https://lnkd.in/gTjGUwkn).

While static decimation in preprocessing reduces data size, on-the-fly sampling (sampling randomly at each epoch) offers a secret weapon: Implicit Data Augmentation. By showing the model different points of the same physics at every pass, we force it to learn the underlying continuous field rather than just memorizing a fixed grid—a concept first championed by AB-UPT. But, as noted in the SMART paper sampling must be robust. (ArGEnt uses random sampling only once and keep it fixed throughout training.) If it’s too aggressive or poorly distributed, we risk missing the small features or local gradients that define the physics.

The pace of development in Transformer-based SciML is breathtaking. (Funny thing is, I won’t be able to keep up without the help of AI - NotebookLM has been really helpful, with audiovisual assistance like the infographic here, and even drafting this very post!) It’s rewarding to see the bottlenecks I face in an industrial setting being dismantled one by one by these evolving architectures.

With the architecture maturing so rapidly, the billion-dollar question shifts: Can our data generation pipelines keep up with the appetite of these new models? Do we really plan to run hundreds of CFD simulations for every single product program?
entry.summary: SciML (Scientific Machine Learning) comes with a unique set of “high-stakes” challenges. One is the sheer cost of data generation—requiring deep CAE expertise, massive compute, and time, as I mentioned in my previous post.

entry.title: AI Grad Student: Research in the age of Agentic AI

link.title: AI Grad Student: Research in the age of Agentic AI

entry.content:
CAE engineers are a unique breed. We are border riders between physics and IT. For us, HPC is a close friend, and we have to understand the infrastructure to do our day job. Every CAE engineer has a personal library of scripts, whether in Python or Bash (and yes, Bash is a programming language!), for populating jobs, monitoring queues, killing processes with specific patterns, and aggregating outputs into CSVs. We’ve always been the masters of our own automation.

Recently, as I’ve gone deeper into Scientific Machine Learning (SciML), VS Code has become my bread and butter. While I admired the context-awareness of tools like Claude Code, I recently learned during a GitHub Copilot Immersive Session that I could achieve that same “agentic” workflow right inside VS Code using copilot-instructions.md.

I defined my bsub wrapper commands and workspace structure (source, data farm, runs) in the instructions file. It took more efforts than I expected. Then yesterday, I finished the instructions file, and gave Copilot a small challenge: “Grab this and that data, create a template YAML pointing to them for training, and populate an ablation study with these specific parameters.”

Without typing a single bsub command, I had 16 jobs active in the queue in seconds. No more “for filename in *.YAML; do vim -e “%s/…/g wq” ; submit_job.sh –config $filename –mode train; done”. Then it showed a summary of submitted jobs, associated LSFIDs with job description, and path to each run, which I asked to save as a markdown and a csv. It is building a complete logbook.

We are moving away from being manual script-writers and toward being Workflow Orchestrators. I’ll be spending my weekend with VS Code and Claude, building a few agents to replicate a viral AI assistant I’ve been following: https://lnkd.in/dEjh-W7i

The Engineering Grammar is changing. Productivity is skyrocketing. Our engineering mindset needs to evolve to keep pace with the speed of AI.
entry.summary: CAE engineers are a unique breed. We are border riders between physics and IT. For us, HPC is a close friend, and we have to understand the infrastructure to do our day job. Every CAE engineer has a personal library of scripts, whether in Python or Bash (and yes, Bash is a programming language!), for populating jobs, monitoring queues, killing processes with specific patterns, and aggregating outputs into CSVs. We’ve always been the masters of our own automation.

entry.title: Attention is All You Need, in SciML too.: Trends in SciML modeling approaches

link.title: Attention is All You Need, in SciML too.: Trends in SciML modeling approaches

entry.content:
How can we efficiently process industry level large-scale CFD meshes in Transformer-based SciML without losing critical physical resolution?

Transformer is gaining significant momentum in SciML applications because it provides excellent long-distance relationship information, which is critical for many subsonic fluid dynamics in the parabolic regime. However, the O(N2) computational complexity has long been a bottleneck for industrial applications with millions of grid points, leading researchers to focus heavily on reducing this computational overhead. While Transolver (https://lnkd.in/gAH9XJdm) handles this by grouping similar points into slices and AB-UPT (https://lnkd.in/gDzbWEpW) effectively scales through randomly sampled supernodes and anchor points , I recently found a publication that provides a very attractive alternative called MSPT (https://lnkd.in/g2M53bkN).

In MSPT, the whole point cloud is split into multiple patches using a ball tree. At first glance, it might sound like a compromise compared to Transolver, which creates slices over relevant points by softmax to ensure a certain level of semantic relevance. In contrast, the patches in MSPT are partitioned based primarily on geometry. But the real innovation lies in how the attention is performed; it is not restricted only to the supernodes. A multi-scale attention is applied such that attention is performed among all cells within a single patch, combined with attention to the supernodes (functionally a cross-attention) and self-attention among those supernodes. The authors claim this unified operation captures both local and long-range relationships exceptionally well, and I can’t wait to try it myself.

On a related note, as Transformers continue to gain more attention in SciML — pun intended — it makes me wonder if decimation should become a standard part of the preprocessing pipeline for industrial applications with multi-million grid points. There is certainly a potential for losing fidelity, but we must remember that high CFD mesh resolution is often a necessary evil required to solve the Navier-Stokes equations numerically. For anything other than solving those governing equations, such high density might actually be an overkill for a surrogate model.

Regarding benchmarking, the authors mentioned they had a difficult time reproducing the results of AB-UPT. In my personal opinion, AB-UPT still possesses unique features such as inherent data augmentation through random sampling, and its branched approach holds so much potential for various industrial applications. With the recent release of the Noether framework (https://lnkd.in/gyrP6hBM) , I hope to see even more rigorous studies exploring the strengths of the AB-UPT architecture in the near future.
entry.summary: How can we efficiently process industry level large-scale CFD meshes in Transformer-based SciML without losing critical physical resolution?
